title: Grammar · English C1
scope: C1 (Cambridge Advanced) adds inversion, cleft sentences, advanced and mixed conditionals with alternative conjunctions, participle clauses, emphatic structures, advanced modality, ellipsis and substitution, nominalisation and discourse markers. Boundary: you produce fluent, well-organised, register-aware text; C2 then refines idiom and stylistic subtlety.

blocks.text: After negative or limiting adverbials at the start of a sentence, the subject and auxiliary invert. Formal and emphatic.

rows: Never have I seen such a thing.
Hardly had I arrived when it started.
Not only did he apologise, but he also paid.
Only after the meeting did I understand.

blocks.text: Split a sentence to emphasise one part, using It is/was … that … or What … is ….
It was John who broke the window. What I need is a holiday.
Alternative conditional links and more formal structures.

rows: I won't go unless you come.
You can stay as long as you are quiet.
If she were to call, tell her I'm out.
Had I known, I would have helped.

blocks.text: Use a present or past participle to shorten a clause, common in writing.
Feeling tired, she went to bed. (= Because she felt tired …)
Built in 1850, the bridge is still in use. (= The bridge, which was built in 1850, …)
Auxiliary do/does/did and fronting add emphasis without changing the basic meaning.
I do appreciate your help. Never did I imagine this. That, I cannot accept.
Omit repeated words (ellipsis) or replace them with so/one/do to avoid repetition.
She can sing and so can I. "Is it raining?" — "I think so." I'd like the red one.
Formal connectors structure argument and contrast in writing.

rows: It was hard; nonetheless, we finished.
He is calm, whereas she is nervous.
She invested early, thereby securing profit.

blocks.text: Turning verbs/adjectives into nouns to create a denser, more formal academic style.
Prices increased rapidly → The rapid increase in prices … · They decided to … → Their decision to …
